figures.caption: Figure 5: Qualitative examples of the position–size stitching variants on nuScenes val. For each scene, we visualize the predicted trajectory before (green) and after (red) stitching a vehicle sprite at four placements: Near and Far on the road, and Sky and SkyFar in the sky region (Far and SkyFar share the same sprite scale). The trajectory visibly bends or shortens for road-placed sprites, while sky-placed sprites of the same size leave the trajectory essentially unchanged, consistent with the quantitative results in Table 12.

Figure 1: Counterfactual obstacle stitching on nuScenes val. Left columns: original front-camera image and the same image with a near-range vehicle sprite stitched in front of the ego. Right columns: BEV trajectories before (green) and after (red) stitching for existing VLAs and our model. While existing VLAs’ trajectory remains nearly invariant to the visual input, whereas our model produces a distinct slowdown/avoidance reaction in response to the stitched obstacle, demonstrating that visual features causally influence its trajectory output.

Figure 2: Existing Driving VLA (a) vs. our model (b). Existing models decode the trajectory directly from the current visual tokens Vt, ego status E, and text command C. In contrast, ours requires the LLM to predict a future visual state V̂t+∆ and decodes the trajectory through an IK Network conditioned only on the pair (Vt, V̂t+∆), so that ego status and text commands do not directly enter the trajectory decoder.

Table 1: PDMS results on the NAVSIM-v1 benchmark. Bold=best, underline=second best. † denotes models trained with the SFT→GRPO schedule. OpenDriveVLA* shares all training hyperparameters, data, and the SFT→GRPO schedule with our model; the only differences are the next visual state prediction objective and the IK Network.
Table 2: EPDMS results on the NAVSIM-v2 benchmark. Bold=best, underline=second best.

Table 16: Inference speed of our 0.5B model on a single NVIDIA RTX A6000, measured on the nuScenes validation set with three front cameras and DDPM 50-step IK sampling. End-to-end is the GPU-only sum (vision encoder + LLM + IK). For context, we list reported latency of representative Driving VLAs and large VLMs.
Table 9: Per-sample analysis of next state prediction’s benefit on nuScenes val. ∗∗∗: p < 0.001, ∗: p < 0.05, n.s.: p ≥ 0.05.

Table 10: Per-sample correlation between Next State Prediction (NSP) quality and trajectory accuracy on nuScenes val split (N = 5,869). NSP MSE is computed in the projected hidden space (896-D); NSP cosine is the mean token-wise cosine similarity. A larger NSP MSE / smaller NSP cosine indicates a worse next-state prediction. ∗∗∗: p < 0.001, ∗∗: p < 0.01, ∗: p < 0.05, n.s.: p ≥ 0.05.
Table 15: Performance on nuScenes-QA. Bold=best, Underline=second.
Table 3: Open-Loop planning performance on nuSenes val set. “-” indicates the metric is not reported for that method in the paper’s tables. Bold=best, Underline=second.

Table 12: Stitching response on nuScenes val split (n = 6019), decomposed by sprite size and position, measured with paired same-seed sampling (K=50 DDIM mean). ∆ is the signed 3 s endpoint displacement (after − before, in meters); |∆| is its magnitude. Far and SkyFar share sprite scale (0.5) and differ only in position (road horizon vs. sky), forming the decisive paired control.
Table 6: Visual grounding via GradCAM–object overlap on nuScenes val set.

Figure 3: Overview of our model. The vision encoder extracts visual tokens Vt from the three front cameras; the LLM consumes (Vt, E, C) together with the QA prompt and is trained to predict the future visual state V̂t+∆ (and to answer driving-scene questions). The Inverse Kinematics Network, a cross-attention conditional diffusion model, decodes the trajectory from only the pair (Vt, V̂t+∆), without direct access to ego status E or text command C.
